segs.utf8:
This episode contains scenes some listeners 
might find upsetting and some scenes in this
series use recreated sound effects.
Some succeed by what they know, some by

what they do and few by what they are.
A large, high-ceilinged room hung with

paintings. At one end of it, two doors flank 
an imposing fireplace. This is the​ ​Roosevelt
Room at the White House.
Clearly our ten finalists
have succeeded in all three categories.
Nasa administrator James Beggs stands at a

podium addressing the invited guests and media.  
To his right is the United States Vice President,

George Bush Senior. To his left is a 
group of six women and four men.

They’ve demonstrated that they’re 
winners. And we’re very proud of
these ten young men and women.
It’s July 1985. And one of these

ten is about to make history.  
When the shuttle programme was first

announced fifteen years earlier it promised 
to make spaceflight routine. So far eighteen

shuttle missions have flown. And the schedule 
is ramping up… there have already been four

launches this year and another five 
are scheduled before 1985 is out.

But there’s a downside to the 
perception that spaceflight is
routine.

​The public and the media are losing 
interest in the shuttle program. The

excitement around the early missions has gone 
and TV networks aren’t even showing launches,

never mind the rest of the missions. The 
space shuttle simply isn’t news anymore.

And that’s a worry for NASA. This lack of 
public and media interest could undermine
political and financial support for the programme.
Well, we’re here today to announce the first private
citizen passenger in the history of spaceflight.
In the Roosevelt Room, Vice President George
Bush is addressing the audience.
The President said last August that this

passenger would be one of America’s finest, 
a teacher.

Well, since then, as we've heard,
Nasa, with the help of the heads

of our state school systems has searched the 
nation for a teacher with the right stuff.
​By sending a teacher into space,

Nasa hopes to get the 
public behind the shuttle programme once again.

So let me tell you now who our Teacher in Space 
will be. And let me say, I thought I was a world
traveller but this tops anything I’ve tried.
And for the White House it’s a chance

to show their support for an 
education system under strain.

And the winner, the teacher who will be 
going into space: Christa McAuliffe.

Thirty-six-year-old Christa McAuliffe teaches 
social studies at Concord High School in

New Hampshire. She radiates enthusiasm 
and energy. She is a star teacher.

She shakes the vice-president’s 
hand and steps up to the podium…

It’s not often that a teacher is at a loss 
for words. I know my students wouldn’t think

so. I’ve made nine wonderful friends over the 
last two weeks. A​nd when that shuttle goes,

there might be one body… but there’s gonna be 
ten souls that I’m taking with me. Thank you.

From the BBC World Service, 13 
Minutes Presents: The Space Shuttle
I’m space scientist Maggie Aderin-Pocock.
Episode 7 of 10: Teacher in Space

Challenger Houston, we have good TEDRIS 
TV. Go ahead with the lesson Christa.

Okay…. Good morning. This is Christa 
McAuliffe live from the Challenger.
McAuliffe is standing in a small cabin surrounded
by consoles and controls.
She’s dressed casually,

wearing a sleeveless sweater with orange and 
blue stripes.

And I'm going to be taking you through
a field trip...
And she’s holding a microphone.

We're going to start out introducing you 
to two very important members of the crew.

The first one is Commander Scobee, who 
is sitting to my left. And the second
one is Michael Smith.
But right now the seats

are empty. It’s just McAuliffe.  
She’s in the crew compartment trainer, ​a

recreation of the space shuttle’s flight deck and 
mid-deck – at Johnson Space Center in Houston.

Now, Commander Scobee is going to 
tell you a little bit about flying

the Orbiter. And Commander Smith or… excuse 
me… pilot Smith is going to be telling you

a little bit about the SPOC which is 
the computer that is used on board.

She is rehearsing a video presentation that 
she’ll be doing from the shuttle during the

mission. She’s expected to do in space 
what she does so brilliantly on Earth.

Okay? (Okay… ready to come down?)
Now when I go downstairs that's going to

move. I'm gonna switch the camera. Okay, and I'm 
going to pick up the handheld mic down here.

When she gives the guided tour of Challenger 
for real – when in space – the hope is she’ll

be watched by hundreds of thousands 
of school students across the US.

We'll talk about the bathroom first? Is that what 
we decided? (Barbara Morgan: Unintelligible) Oh…

handheld mic. (Barbara Morgan: Unintelligible). 
 
That other voice you can hear in the

background – helping McAuliffe plan the 
presentation - is Barbara Morgan. She’s

been selected as the back-up for the Teacher 
in Space programme - If McAuliffe isn’t able
to fly for any reason, Morgan will step in.

I applied because 
I'm a teacher. And as teachers,

we're always looking for ways to further our own 
education to make the classroom more interesting,

engaging, exciting place, and to bring the world 
into our classroom and knock those four walls
down and take our students out into the world.
Nasa is a very different environment to the school

in Idaho where Morgan works.
​​ ​

The two teachers have an intensive 
schedule over several months.

They need to learn as much as they can about the 
shuttle. They need to work together to prepare the

lessons that McAuliffe will teach in space. 
And they need to get to know the rest of the

crew… sometimes in the back of 
a high-performance aircraft…

So our commander Dick Scobee and our pilot Mike 
Smith took Christa and me out flying for the

very first time on the most exciting flights 
that either of us had ever had in our lives.

The two teachers are each sitting 
in the back of a T-38 jet.

The T-38s are airforce jet trainers. They go 
about 500 miles an hour. They’re dual-cockpit,
so you got the front-seater and the back-seater.

And it was to have some experience 
in high performance jet aircraft,

before Christa would fly in the most high 
performing technological flying machine that

we have… our space shuttles. And also to for us 
to learn to trust and understand our commander

and pilot and for our Commander and pilot to 
trust and understand it in a you know in in a

high performance aircraft that we would we would 
be able to be trusted to do the right things.

Christa McAuliffe is in Dick Scobee’s T-38 
jet, the commander of their mission. And

Morgan is flying with Mike Smith 
who will be Challenger’s pilot.

Scobee and Smith settle into formation with 
their aircraft almost wingtip-to-wingtip.

I could literally see Christa’s 
eyes in the plane next to me. And

that gave me a great appreciation 
for just how talented our pilots are,

who fly these kinds of planes…  
 And then we split apart. Dick and Christa went

off flying their way. And Mike and I went flying 
off our way this is over the Gulf of Mexico.

As they’re flying over the ocean, Mike 
Smith shows her how to do barrel rolls…

…take the stick and you push it either all the 
way to the right or all the way to the left so
that the airplane is rolling.
And lazy eights…

… you actually take the plane and you 
fly upwards, and so you draw with your
airplane an eight. A figure eight.
Performing aerobatics and pulling G’s

in fighter jets over the Gulf of Mexico… it’s a 
very different day at work for the two teachers.

Then I heard Mike say to me, okay, Barb, your turn 
to push the stick. And I said, which way and he

said, well, any way you want, just push the stick 
and see what happens. And I was really shocked,

and not expected to do anything like that. And, 
you know, I didn't know how to fly. I didn't have

any confidence in myself. And, but, but Mike had 
the confidence in me and he was able to instil

that confidence by saying, take it, push yourself, 
go for it, see what happens. And, and so I pushed

the stick and did a bunch of barrel rolls and 
flew in long, sweeping arcs high over the Gulf

of Mexico. And Mike opened up a whole, a whole 
new door of opportunities for me by doing that.

It was just a beautiful, bright, bright blue, 
clear sky.. I remember saying something like,

gosh, Mike, this is an incredible view. He said, 
here's a better view for you. And before he

even finished getting those words out, we were 
flying upside down. And here the whole earth,

the whole green and brown of Texas, was 
filling my canopy. And here I was looking up,

up out of the canopy at our Earth, and it 
flipped that whole perspective for me.

There are other opportunities 
for McAuliffe and Morgan to

fly – and they’re just as exhilarating… 
 
NASA’s KC-135 aircraft gives trainee astronauts

the experience of weightlessness. 
It’s known as the Vomit Comet.

It performs a series of parabolas – flying 
up, then down in a U-shaped curve.

So you know, just very much like going over the, 
the top of the hill on a roller coaster. And for

anybody that's been on a roller coaster, when you 
go over the top of that hill, and you feel like

your stomach maybe jumps up a little bit, you are 
actually experiencing, you know, a teeny, teeny

tiny split second or so of weightlessness. And 
that's what happens when you fly in this aircraft.

As you go into that first parabola, you don't have 
to do anything, just sit there and enjoy it. And

then you will slowly lift off the floor. That's 
what we were told. And you'll be floating. And so

that's what I was expecting. And we went into 
that first pull up parabola, and over the top,

and I literally shot to the ceiling, and 
just started laughing. And I don't think
Christa and I quit laughing the entire flight.
McAuliffe and Morgan are relishing the experience

of simulated weightlessness. They try dancing 
in mid-air and leapfrogging over each other.
First leapfrog in zero gravity! One leapfrog…

two leapfrog… three leapfrog…  
In the air, in the classroom,

and in the simulators… McAuliffe and 
Morgan are living and working alongside

each other day after day. They form a bond.  
​Christa was wonderful. She was like the girl

next door. She had a very engaging smile. She was 
quiet. And friendly and just soaking it all in.

But, behind the scenes, among the astronaut 
corps, not everybody is comfortable with the

idea of a private citizen going into space.   
I was not a fan of passengers flying on the

space shuttle. I felt that the 
vehicle wasn’t an airliner.

I understood and thought it was a 
good thing to try to fly different

people in space. But I thought it was 
a little bit early to be doing that.

Mike Mullane and Anna Fisher joined 
NASA as part of the 35 New Guys.

I understood why that was important to give 
to show that the young people that hey,

you know, think big… think of going into space 
someday. Here's a teacher that's up there in

space, I can understand that….  
But space is a hostile environment,

and the shuttle is a hugely complex machine. 
Astronauts like Anna Fisher and Mike Mullane

put their lives on the line when they fly. 
They understand – and accept - the risk.

I didn't know Christa McAuliffe, the 
teacher. But I passed her many times

in the hallways. And whenever I saw her, 
she had an incandescent smile on her face,

just and I wore that smile, too. But I knew the 
risks and the other people that were flying,

knew the risks and, and I thought to myself, 
has anybody sat down with that woman and told

her that this is not as safe as an airliner?  
I know Dick Scobee was concerned about Christa

McAuliffe, because he wasn’t certain that 
she really understood that from the selection

process and all that for the teacher in space.  
As commander of the mission that will take her

into space, Dick Scobee is determined to support 
McAuliffe, and that includes making sure she

understands what she is getting herself into.  
He told me that he made certain that she

understood the risks she was taking. So I know 
he told her. You know sometimes you only hear

what you want to hear so I don’t know, but I’m 
sure he tried to make her aware of the risk.

On the 30th of October, 1985, the space shuttle 
launches from Kennedy on mission STS-61-A – the

two teachers watch from the viewing area.  
The orbiter for this mission is Challenger – the

vehicle that will take McAuliffe and her crew 
into space in less than three months’ time.

But before that happens, Challenger will undergo 
a lot more than just its return from space.

The shuttle was designed to be reused, yes… 
but because of how complex it is… it needs to

be dismantled… and then rebuilt… before it can be 
sent back up to space. It’s a demanding schedule,

with no room for errors. 
Wheel stop. – Roger Wheel

Stop Challenger. Welcome home and 
congratulations on a beautiful flight.

Seven days after its launch the 
orbiter lands successfully at

Edwards Air Force Base in California.  
After a few days, it is flown back to

Kennedy Space Centre in Florida, to 
an orbiter processing facility.

This is when the turnaround 
for the next mission begins.

So we would dismantle the vehicle, 
you know, fairly extensively. The

main engines went back into the engine 
shop. That was a big big challenge.

Shuttle processing engineer John Tribe has been 
with the programme since the very beginning.

All the stuff that comes back from the previous 
mission has to be brought off the vehicle. Every

subsystem has to be tested completely, the vehicle 
has to be configured for the next mission. All the

relevant equipment has to be put into the payload 
bay. The payload itself has to be installed.

After more than a month, Challenger is transported 
to the vehicle assembly building, the VAB – a

structure so tall it has its own weather. On humid 
days, rainclouds can form near its ceiling.

Very much just big-time history here in this 
building. I mean, built in the early 60s.

Bill Carr joined the shuttle program as a 
technician. Now, in late 1985, he’s Pad Leader,

preparing Launch Pad 39B for its first shuttle 
launch: the teacher in space mission.

If you’re 
ever on the roof, you’ve never been that high

before in any building, I’ve been there, 
several times. A lot of history here.

When Challenger arrives, the other elements of 
the shuttle are already here. The huge orange

external tank has been flown over from Louisiana. 
And the two powerful solid rocket boosters have

arrived from their manufacturer in Utah. Together, 
these components form the whole space shuttle,

also called the “shuttle stack”. 
So the external tank would be mated to the

boosters, and now it's ready for the Orbiter. 
Now it’s Challenger’s turn to get

connected – using a very large crane.  
It's probably one of the most sophisticated

computerised cranes of its time.  
Imagine Challenger hanging vertically,

in sort of a metal sling, held only by long 
cables from the crane’s arm far, far above it.

The orbiter weighs 100 tonnes, and it’s worth 
around two billion dollars. So, as it’s moved

towards the rest of the shuttle stack ​it’s really 
important that it doesn’t start swinging.

So if you look at where the crane cab is, 
if you can look up, and when you make one

movement of the stick up at the very top of 
that crane cab, the movement on the pendulum

down here can be significant. So the amount 
of training that goes in for the crane crew

guys… these guys are super specialised. 
Challenger is moving at a snail’s pace,

until it’s right above the external tank and solid 
rocket boosters. Now, the crane operator’s job is

to lower it – very carefully – making sure 
it is in exactly the right position.

As the orbiter slowly descends, it passes 
several platforms with additional workers.

We had guys with pushpads on each platform 
to make sure that if you had to make any

nudges against that thermal protection, that 
you would make small incremental moves.

Everyone’s communicating via radio. If 
anybody, at any point, notices something wrong,

they can call a stop immediately… 
So if anybody called a halt you had

any problem with any kind of movement you 
hit a stop. Then the crane automatically

stops and we would talk about it … 
But it all goes well. Challenger is

successfully attached to the external tank.  
Now, the complete shuttle stack is powered up,

and checked over. Then, a crawler transporter 
moves the shuttle to the launch pad.

It inches forward along caterpillar 
tracks twice the height of a person.

The crawler starts to slow, three 
quarters up to one mile an hour out

to the launch pad. …I think that 
took the better part of the day…
​I think we

would roll out at midnight, would be hard down 
at noon or whatever out there at the pad.

It’s taken a month and a half to get it ready. 
But finally, the job is done. Challenger sits

waiting on launchpad 39B. 
The early visions of the

shuttle compared it to a commercial airliner. 
You know, the original goal was I can remember

back in the early 70s 160 hour turnaround.  
Shuttle processing engineer John Tribe.

In other words, you should get the vehicle back 
land, roll it into the hangar and have it out

ready to launch again in 160 hours and launch 
40 times a year. Well, even us old timers then

said We ain't ever gonna do that. So it took I 
think on the first couple of flights six months

to get the vehicle back from the flight and ready 
for the next flight. Ready to roll out again.

The shuttle team has become faster since 
those early flights. But the turnaround

is clearly far more complex than a simple 
clean and refuel. And now, a few years into

the programme, the flight rate is ramping up. 
‘85 and ‘86 were busy years. And we were starting

to really get a lot of pressure on 
increasing the launch rate. Probably

one of the worst times of our lives. 
The flight rate was pushing us pretty hard.

Since the first shuttle mission, STS-1, 
Bob Crippen has commanded three more.

We had proved the basics of what we wanted 
the space shuttle to be able to do, and

we were proud of the way it was behaving. ​ 
​But we also had some issues with the vehicle

that we were continuing to work with… 
But the vehicle didn't always come back

clean. We had various times when we came 
back in and we had landing gear problems,

brakes tires, we did have some heat 
damage on various various tiles.

So trying to correct all those. At the same time 
trying to push the flight rate was a challenge.

As 1985 draws to a close, the teams working on the 
space shuttle have managed to launch more missions

than any year so far – a total of nine.  
It’s an unrelenting schedule. And in 1986,

there are fifteen flights planned. That means 
that there’s very little time for the teams

to get on top of the issues they can see… 
never mind the ones which lie deeper.

In early 1986, seven astronauts arrive 
at Kennedy Space Center in Florida.

Once they’re on the ground, ​each member of the 
crew says a few words to the waiting media.

Commander Dick Scobee speaks first… 
As usual, it's a real pleasure to be at

the Cape to come down here and participate 
in something that the Cape does better than

anybody in the world. And that's launching 
space vehicles.
Then he introduces Pilot Mike Smith...

I'm glad to have him on his 
board as a pilot, so here’s Mike.

Thanks, Dick, I'm one of the three people on 
board who's, who's in, it'll be my first time to

fly and we're just all looking forward to getting 
on over and getting the secret handshake.

Next up is mission specialist Judy Resnik. 
Well, I too, am glad to be here one more time.

She is one of the six women recruited 
as part of the 35 New Guys.

And I will now introduce El Onizuka. 
Let me say that it's really a pleasure to be

back. Looking forward to going and fly this one. 
Ellison Onizuka is from the same astronaut class,
and the first Asian-American astronaut.

We're looking forward to it. And 
I think we're ready to go fly.

Next is Ron McNair.
Again, I'd like to echo

the opinions of my crew members that we look 
forward to launching from the Cape first of all,

and returning here a few days later. 
He’s also part of the 35 New Guys and one

of NASA’s first African-American astronauts. 
His first mission was the one that released

the two satellites that were stranded in space. 
It's a great pleasure finally to get this far.

Greg Jarvis was due to fly on the shuttle 
mission which launched two weeks earlier.

But his place on that flight was taken by a US 
Congressman and he was bumped to this mission.

I am very proud to be part of 
the programme that NASA has
put together and I'm glad to be representing...
But the nation’s

attention today is focused on 
Christa McAuliffe… the Teacher in

Space. Wearing her blue NASA flight suit 
she steps forward to the microphone.

Well I am so excited to be here. I don't 
think any teacher has ever been more ready

to have two lessons in my life. I've 
been preparing these September. And I

just hope everybody tunes in on day four to 
watch the teacher teaching from space.

This is Shuttle Launch Control. Commander 
Dick Scobee getting into the orbiter,

followed by pilot Mike Smith.  
In the four days since the press

conference, Challenger’s crew have been in 
final preparations for the mission and said

good bye to their families and loved 
ones. Launch was originally scheduled

yesterday, but was delayed due to 
unfavourable weather conditions.

Mission specialist Judy Resnick now in the 
orbiter and about her communication checks,

while teacher payload specialist Christa McAuliffe 
has put on her egress harness and will be just

about to put on her launch & entry helmet. 
One by one, each member of the crew gets ready,

then enters through a round 
hatch at the side of the orbiter.

I was out at the pad, as an ops guy. As they 
close the hatch. It was a beautiful day.

Pad leader Bill Carr is looking forward to 
a successful first launch from pad 39B.

We’re having a little difficulty… 
But there’s a problem.

A piece of equipment known as the “milk 
stool” is attached to the hatch.
It's not to go fly, it's so you can grab it and

close the hatch. And you don't 
touch the flight hardware.

Once the hatch is shut, the close-out 
crew have to remove the nuts holding the

milk stool. But the thread on ​one of the 
nuts is stripped. They can’t get it off.

Go ahead.
Okay, do we

have the proper tools up there to do the job.
That’s a negative. I do not have the drill.

The close out crew up in the White Room 
have requested additional tools and

which will be brought out to the pad 
So I sent a bunch of drill motors and

batteries out to the pad. Unfortunately, the 
batteries had been sitting for a while so they

snapped the battery in the drill when it was 
under a load. These batteries were failing,

which we didn't know they'd been 
sitting on charge for too long.

So the closeout crew leader, running out 
of options, asks if he can use a hacksaw

to literally saw the nut plate off.. 
Well, naturally, they said “no hacksaws”

on national TV. You've got the cameras 
and imagine them sawing something off

the space shuttle before it launches.  
While the closeout crew wrestle with the handle,

the countdown has stayed paused at the T-9 
minute hold. But to extend the window for launch,

the team in the control room make the decision to 
go back to the previous hold at T-20 minutes.

They reset the Ground Launch Sequencer 
or GLS, ​the computer which controls the

final phase leading to launch.  
So I remember Roberta Wyrick was

the test conductor. And I she had said, you 
know on my mark, we're going to reset GLS –

On my mark three… two… one… mark GLS. 
Okay, we've reset. And just seconds later

Junior says we've got the milk stool off. 
The closeout crew have solved the problem.

But it’s too late. For hours there’s been 
good weather. But now the crosswinds have

increased. And they’re too strong to launch.  
 We are going to scrub for today and the people

will be coming out to the pad to 
let the crew out of the orbiter.

​And that always haunted me because that was a 
very nice day the crew was all strapped in and

I'm sure we would have had a successful mission. 
Good morning this is Shuttle Launch Control at

T-minus three hours and holding.  
It’s the next day. Early morning

on 28th January 1986.  
The countdown of launching

mission 51 l is proceeding smoothly.
The crew is still sleeping at this time

they will be awakened in just a short while at 
6:20am and they will then have breakfast and

on their flight clothing. Departure for the pad. 
Pad B is scheduled for about 10 minutes of eight.

That night, the weather was just abysmal.  
Shuttle processing engineer John Tribe.

You know, it was the one of the coldest 
nights I've ever experienced in Florida.

​​It was down in the low 20s. 
That’s in Fahrenheit.

Around -7 degrees Celsius.  
I grew up in Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania you know

it can be very cold. I'd never seen anything like 
this. I mean, I went in and I had it. I didn't

even have the kind of jacket that I would need to 
wear in Florida. So I borrowed a jacket from my
dad and I bundled up because it was so cold.
​Spectators

usually gather many hours ahead of a launch. 
The roads near Kennedy Space Center would be

crowded with cars and families – even before 
dawn - as they wait excitedly for lift-off.

But not this morning. The roads are quiet. 
It’s just too cold to stand waiting. Those who

have arrived are sitting in their vehicles with 
engines running, doing their best to stay warm.

I came in at three or four o'clock 
in the morning, and we go to do the

final walk down. And there's ice everywhere.  
Launchpad 39B resembles an eerie winter scene.

The pad is covered in long icicles.  
Great, great sheets of ice everywhere.

If you ever look at any, any photographs, it looks 
something out of the Ice House in Dr. Zhivago. It

was you know, it's just awful. Ice everywhere.  
The evening before, John Tribe had been keeping an

eye on the conditions, when it was clear 
it would be an incredibly cold night.

I remember walking back across to the office, 
and I passed a group of my guys that were going

over there and they said, What What's the 
status? I said, we're not flying. Nobody. I

said no way we can fly with that condition out 
there. There wasn’t even a doubt in my mind.

But not everyone shares Tribe’s certainty. 
We had analysts at Downey, analysts at Houston

trying to figure out where will the ice go 
when it lifts off? Is there any damage or

any threat to the vehicle and they finally 
convinced themselves that that there would

be no threat. And we'd go for launch.  
Filling of the shuttle Challenger’s external

tank with its flight load of liquid 
hydrogen and liquid oxygen was delayed

this morning, and was started at about 4am. 
While the shuttle’s external tank is being filled,

an ice team looks at conditions on 
the launch pad. The concern is that

icicles could break off during launch and damage 
the fragile tiles of Challenger’s heatshield.

I went over to a point-to-point phone. I went 
straight to the control room, talked to the

backup Test Conductor. And I said this, Pat is I 
mean, has anybody been out here? And I describe

all the ice. So the backup test conductor says, 
okay, we'll notify the crew office to let them

know to be extra careful. And I thought to myself, 
this isn't the desired result I'm looking for.

For Bill Carr this isn’t about slipping 
on the ice. It’s about whether Challenger

should be launching at all.   
There's a lot of attention to

STS 51-Ll because of the folks that 
were flying on it more than anything,

most notably to Christa McAuliffe, a teacher in 
space and long awaited ballyhooed presidential

involvement. It was it was a big deal. 
In Mission Control, astronaut Dick Covey

is at his console. He is the “Capcom” during 
Challenger’s ascent into orbit. He’s the only

member of the team who communicates directly with 
the crew during launch. And over the last few

months he’s been working very closely with them. 
They know me, they know my voice. They know I'm

the guy talking to them. I know the things 
I'm supposed to say they have that level of

say comfort with who's talking to them and 
what they're saying from the control centre.

Launch has been delayed by a couple of 
hours to allow the ice team to assess

the situation and remove more icicles 
from the pad. It’s now set for 11:38.
Challenger Houston, good morning.
​On launch pad 39B the astronauts

are strapped in. They’re all wearing gloves. Even 
inside the shuttle it’s difficult to keep warm.

Challenger… Houston. For all crew members… 
how do you read? Simul voice check.

Dick Scobee is in the commander’s seat on 
the left of Challenger’s flight deck.

CDR reads you loud and clear… 
Mike Smith, the pilot, is on the right.

PLT reads you loud and clear… 
Behind them sit mission specialists

Ellison Onizuka… 
MS1 loud and clear…

…and Judy Resnik. 
MS2 loud and clear…

They lie on their backs looking through
